post.author: Artshin
post.caption: 1.2K2112Artshin · FollowingArtshin · Original audioThe glass nearly shattered before anyone understood why. One second, Preston was sitting at the center of the sunlit luxury terrace like a king in his wheelchair, surrounded by crystal glasses, polished marble, and rich people laughing over champagne. The next—an eight-year-old barefoot boy landed прямо on his table. Glasses shook. Women screamed. Phones flew up. And in a voice so sharp it cut through the whole terrace, the boy pointed at him and said, “Sir… I can fix your leg.” For half a second, no one moved. Then the laughter exploded. Loud, cruel, effortless. Someone shouted, “Throw him out!” Preston leaned back slowly, amused, arrogant, enjoying the spectacle the way men like him enjoy anything they think they control. He pulled out his checkbook, laid it on the table, and smirked. “You? Do it… and I’ll give you a million.” More laughter. But the boy didn’t smile. Didn’t argue. He simply jumped down from the table and walked straight to Preston’s wheelchair as if the crowd didn’t exist. That was what made the laughter weaken. He dropped to his knees. Placed one small hand gently on Preston’s leg. Looked up with frightening calm. “Count with me.” Preston scoffed. “This is ridicu—” Then he stopped. Completely. His face changed first. Not belief. Not yet. Shock. Because under the expensive fabric of his trousers, his leg twitched. Just once. Small. But real. The entire terrace went dead silent. Even the jazz felt far away. Phones trembled in raised hands. The boy whispered, “One…” Preston’s breathing changed. His leg moved again—stronger this time. He slammed both palms against the table, sending silverware rattling. “What did you do?!” he shouted, and now there was no mockery left in him. Only fear. A glass slipped from someone’s hand and shattered on the marble. The boy slowly lifted his eyes to him. Calm. Knowing. “Two…” Preston gripped the table so hard his knuckles turned white. Then—impossibly—he pushed upward. The wheelchair creaked beneath him. For one terrible, beautiful second, hope flooded his face. Raw. Childlike. Then it vanished just as fast, replaced by something darker when he looked down at the boy and whispered, “Who are you?!” The child leaned closer. So close his lips nearly touched Preston’s ear. And whatever he whispered made all the blood drain from the millionaire’s face. His mouth opened. His eyes widened in pure horror. Because suddenly this wasn’t about a miracle anymore. It was about recognition. About a secret. About someone Preston thought had been buried years ago. He began to rise from the wheelchair—shaking, terrified—while the entire terrace watched in stunned silence… and then his lips trembled as he looked down at the boy and whispered, “No… that’s impossible…” Part 2 in the comments. article.title: PART 2: “Sir… I can help your leg.”
article.content:
Laughter fills the terrace—soft music drifting under golden lights, glasses clinking, wealthy guests relaxed—

Camera settles on Preston in his wheelchair, raising his wine glass—smiling—

then—

a small barefoot boy steps into frame.

Close.

Too close.

“Sir… I can help your leg.”

The laughter spreads instantly.

Some guests turn.

Others smirk.

Preston looks him up and down—amused.

“You? How long will that take?”

The boy doesn’t hesitate.

“Just a few seconds.”

More laughter.

Phones begin to rise.

Preston leans forward slightly—cold now—

places a checkbook on the table.

“Fix it… I’ll give you a million.”

The air shifts.

Laughter fades.

Something heavier replaces it.

The boy steps closer.

Slow.

Unafraid.

He kneels beside the chair.

Gently places his hand on Preston’s leg.

The music dips—

lower—

darker—

“Count with me.”

Preston smirks again—about to dismiss him—

“This is ridicu—”

He stops.

Mid-word.

His breath catches.

CLOSE-UP—

his foot.

A twitch.

Small.

But real.

His eyes widen instantly.

“…what…?”

The terrace goes silent.

Guests lean forward.

Phones trembling now.

The boy’s voice stays steady.

“One… two…”

The leg moves again.

Stronger this time.

Preston grips the table hard.

Breathing changes.

Faster.

Uncontrolled.

He tries to push himself up—

hands shaking—

hope breaking through fear—

Camera PUSHES IN—

his face collapsing between disbelief and something dangerous—real—

—and just as he begins to rise—
